Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - A GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL ON DIS - CRETE DISTRIBUTIONS AND ITS APPLICATION IN TWO SAMPLE TEST

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We propose a generalized probability kernel(GPK) on discrete distributions with finite support. This probability kernel, defined as kernel between distributions instead of samples, generalizes the existing discrepancy statistics such as maximum mean discrepancy(MMD) as well as probability product kernels, and extends to more general cases. For both existing and newly proposed statistics, we estimate them through empirical frequency and illustrate the strategy to analyze the resulting bias and convergence bounds. We further propose power-MMD, a natural extension of MMD in the framework of GPK, illustrating its usage for the task of two-sample test. Our work connects the fields of discrete distribution-property estimation and kernel-based hypothesis test, which might shed light on more new possibilities.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

We focus on the two-sample problem, which is given two i.i.d samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ...xn} , {y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., yn}_ , could we infer the discrepancy between underlying distributions they are drawn from. For such a problem, the option of hypothesis test(two-sample test) is most popular, and a variety of statistics in estimating the discrepancy is proposed. In recent years, RKHS based method such as maximum mean discrepancy(MMD) has gained a lot of attention. (Gretton et al., 2012) has shown that in a universal-RKHS _F_ , MMD( _F,_ _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = 0 if and only if _**p**_ = _**q**_ , thus could be used for the two-sample hypothesis test. (Gretton et al., 2012) further provides unbiased estimator of MMD with fast asymptotic convergence rate, illustrating its advantages.

On the other hand, estimating distribution properties with plugin(empirical) estimators on discrete setting is an active research area in recent years, where people focus on problem settings with large support size but not so large sample size. The Bernstein polynomial technique is introduced to analyze the bias of the plugin estimators in (Yi & Alon, 2020), which provides remarkable progress on bias-reduction methods of the plugin estimators. It is thus interesting to ask if the plugin estimators could motivate new results for the RKHS-based two-sample test.

Another interesting topic is about the probability kernel, defined as kernel function over probabilities, instead of over samples. As is easily seen, any discrepancy measure of distribution _**p**_ and _**q**_ could potentially be valid probability kernels, not so much work focuses on this. While (Jebara et al., 2004) introduced the so called probability product kernels which generalize a variety of discrepancy measures, its properties remain further study.

Motivated by above observations, our work focuses on a specialized probability kernel function which is a direct generalization of sample-based RKHS methods such as MMD. We focus on using plugin-estimator as the default estimator of the kernel function we defined, and illustrate that with the help of Bernstein polynomial techniques, we could analyze the bias and convergence bounds of these plugin-estimators. Our work thus connects the fields of discrete distribution-property estimation and kernel-based hypothesis test, which brings interesting possibilities.

## 3 GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL

Probability kernel function, defined as kernel function between distributions instead of samples, is a natural extension of the idea of kernel function in sample space.

**Definition 1.** _Given distribution_ _**p** and_ _**q** belongs to a family of discrete distribution with the same finite support {v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}, vi ∈_ R _[d] , where k is the support size, we define the probability kernel function as_ PK( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _, which is a kernel function maps from_ R _[k] ×_ R _[k] to real value_ R _._

Many discrepancy measures, such as MMD, can serve as probability kernel functions, but people usually don’t use the term of probability kernel function when describing them. The reason is that for most of the time, we only consider a limited number of distributions, and do not need or have the resources to navigate through all the distributions within the family. For example, when looking into the two-sample problem, we usually assume two samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ..., xn} ∈_ R _[d]_ and _{y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., yn} ∈_ R _[d]_ are i.i.d drawn from two distributions _**p**_ and _**q**_ , and use the discrepancy measure MMD[ _F,_ _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ] to determine if _**p**_ and _**q**_ are indistinguishable in the RKHS _F_ . We do not consider all other distributions in _F_ that is irrelevant to our samples! So far the idea of kernel function between distributions is in practice not so much useful, however, here in this paper, we propose, when considering the plugin-estimator of many of the existing discrepancy measures, it is beneficial to view them as probability kernel functions.

## 3.1.3 EXAMPLE 3: POWER-MMD AS MEMBERS OF POLYNOMIAL GPK

Another interesting subset of polynomial GPK is the one extends MMD case into a power form and we denote it as power-MMD:

Apparently, MMD is a special case of power-MMD where _ρ_ = 1, and power-MMD satisfies the requirement in Theorem 1, thus has the potential usage of discrepancy measure. In section 5., we will show that power-MMD has unbiased estimator with analyzable convergence bounds thus could be used for two-sample test.

## 3.2 DISCUSSION OF GPK IN DISCRETE SETTING

As one may easily notice, the definition of GPK includes a gram matrix generated by the kernel function RK( _vi, vj_ ) which measures the discrepancy between _vi, vj ∈{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, .., vk}_ . While considering the cases of categorical distribution, values of discrete variables does not relate to any notion of distance, this raises the question: how the introduced gram matrix will be beneficial in any cases?

The answer is twofold: 1. Many natural processes produce discrete distributions where there possibly exists a similarity measure in values which imply the similarity in frequencies of occurrence(probability values). For example, in the field of natural language process(NLP), one may treat words as atomic units with no notion of similarity between words, as these are represented as indices in a vocabulary. However, given large number of training samples, similarity measure between words could be made possible using techniques such as words2vec(Mikolov et al., 2013). Such techniques generally result in better performance and have become the important preprocessing techniques for NLP tasks(Goodfellow et al., 2016). 2. As there are cases where the values of discrete variables are totally irrelevant, or people may use kernel function RK( _vi, vj_ ) which doesn’t correctly imply the similarity in probability values, the GPK framework may still capture the similarity between distributions. One example is the case of MMD, which is, as we discussed above, an element of GPK family. As proved in (Gretton et al., 2012), MMD is a distribution free measurement between two samples, which means no matter what kind of _**p** ,_ _**q**_ and kernel( _x, y_ ), we have, the MMD[2] _F_[(] _**[p]**[,]_ _**[ q]**_[)][ measure will be][ 0][ if and only if] _**[ p]**_[=] _**[q]**_[.][However, the bad choice of kernel function] does have a negative effect on convergence bounds of the empirical estimator proposed in (Gretton et al., 2012), and will influence the results of two-sample test. For this reason, we mainly focus on dataset with known relativity measures in our experiment section.

## 4 PLUGIN-ESTIMATOR FOR GPK

We illustrate that by doing so, the techniques of Bernstein polynomial in (Qian et al., 2011) could be used to help building unbiased estimators for any members of polynomial GPK. Furthermore, we provide analyzable convergence bounds of these estimators.

## 6 SUMMARY

To summarize, we introduce the framework of generalized probability kernel(GPK). While GPK represents a large family of probability kernels, we focus on polynomial GPK since all members of such subset of GPK have unbiased plugin-estimators. Remarkably, a natural extension of MMD from the viewpoint of polynomial GPK, which we call power-MMD, could be used for two-sample test. Theoretical study shows that for _ρ_ = 1 case, power-MMD outperforms linear time MMD proposed in Gretton et al. (2012), and the performance of _ρ >_ 1 case is left for future work. For members of GPK which do not belong to polynomial GPK, it is not easy to design an unbiased estimators. However, bais reduction techniqes proposed in (Yi et al., 2018) and (Yi & Alon, 2020) could be used, and we still have the chance to apply two-sample test with the resulting estimators. Such a possibility is also left for future work.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a theoretically grounded and conceptually novel contribution by introducing the Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK), a framework that unifies and extends several existing kernel-based methods for discrete distributions, including Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and probability product kernels. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with precise mathematical definitions and a logical progression from motivation to formalization. The connection between discrete distribution estimation via plug-in estimators and kernel methods is well-motivated and timely, particularly through the use of Bernstein polynomial techniques to analyze bias and convergence—this bridges two important lines of work in statistics and machine learning. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues that affect its overall quality and reproducibility. Most notably, there are numerous typographical errors and formatting artifacts—such as broken subscripts (_{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ...xn}), malformed expressions ([T] =[]), and inconsistent notation—that severely hinder readability and suggest incomplete proofreading or formatting problems in LaTeX compilation. These issues raise concerns about the rigor of presentation, even if the underlying ideas are sound. Furthermore, while the GPK is proposed as a generalization of MMD and related methods, the paper lacks a clear comparative analysis or empirical validation (even in the full version, if included) demonstrating the advantages of GPK over existing approaches in practical or theoretical settings. The discussion of "power-MMD" remains underdeveloped, with limited insight into how it improves upon classical MMD in hypothesis testing power or sample efficiency. Overall, the conceptual contribution is promising and the mathematical framework appears sound, but the paper would benefit significantly from thorough revisions to fix technical formatting issues, clarify key derivations, and strengthen the motivation through either theoretical comparisons or empirical illustrations.

But kernels are typically non-negative, and discrepancy measures like MMD are defined as squared norms or distances. Crucially, the expression as written would be negative unless absolute value or square is taken. However, the sum expansion in the general GPK definition shows a double sum over i,j of φ(pi,qi) K_ij φ(qj,pj), and for power-MMD, this becomes sum_{i,j} (pi^ρ − qi^ρ) K_ij (qj^ρ − pj^ρ) = − sum_{i,j} (pi^ρ − qi^ρ) K_ij (pj^ρ − qj^ρ), which is −(Δ^ρ)^T K Δ^ρ, where Δ^ρ = p^ρ − q^ρ. Since K is PSD, this is ≤ 0. But a discrepancy measure should be ≥ 0 and zero iff p=q. Thus, unless a negative sign is intended (unlikely), this is a **major error**. The only resolution is that the mapping should be φ(p,q) = p^ρ − q^ρ, but then GPK should be φ(p,q)^T K φ(p,q), not φ(p,q)^T K φ(q,p). Or, φ(q,p) is defined as p^ρ − q^ρ as well (symmetric). But the GPK definition uses φ(q,p), which for anti-symmetric φ would flip sign. Looking at condition in Theorem 1, φ(p,q) = −φ(q,p) is allowed. So if φ(p,q) = p^ρ − q^ρ, then φ(q,p) = q^ρ − p^ρ = −φ(p,q), satisfying antisymmetry. Then GPK = φ(p,q)^T K φ(q,p) = φ(p,q)^T K (−φ(p,q)) = −φ(p,q)^T K φ(p,q) ≤ 0. Still negative! However, the L2 norm condition ∥φ ◦ (**p**, **q**)∥₂ = 0 iff **p**=**q** is satisfied because pi^ρ − qi^ρ = 0 for all i iff pi=qi. But the proof of Theorem 1 still fails due to K not being PD. So while the claim about power-MMD being a discrepancy kernel may still be true under stronger assumptions, it depends on both the injectivity of x^ρ and the definiteness of K, which is not fully justified. The use of ρ ∈ Z⁺ is acceptable, but the link to Theorem 1 is fragile.

comments.title: Gaps in Justification for MMD as GPK in Example 1
comments.quote: we can rewrite MMD with distribution probability function pi, qi : Where φl ◦ (**p**, **q**) = **p** − **q** , H is the RKHS defined in MMD literature, and f is the function that maps vi to H .
comments.explanation: The passage claims MMD can be rewritten in terms of pi, qi and φl(**p**, **q**) = **p** − **q**, but provides no derivation or reference. Standard MMD is written as E[f(x)] − E[f(y)] in an RKHS, which becomes ∑_i f(v_i)(p_i − q_i), and ||E[f(x)] − E[f(y)]||² = (∑_i (p_i − q_i) k(v_i, ⋅))^T K^{-1}? No — actually, in the finite-dimensional case, MMD² = (∑_i p_i k(v_i, ⋅) − ∑_j q_j k(v_j, ⋅), ...) = ∑_{i,j} (p_i − q_i) K_{ij} (p_j − q_j) = (**p** − **q**)^T K (**p** − **q**). That matches the GPK definition with φ(pi, qi) = pi − qi. So MMD² = GPK_{F,φ}(**p**,**q**) with φ(pi,qi)=pi−qi. But the paper defines φ ◦ (**p**,**q**) = **p** − **q**, and writes GPK = (φ ◦ (**p**,**q**))^T K (φ ◦ (**q**,**p**)), but if φ is symmetric (φ(**p**,**q**) = **p**−**q**, φ(**q**,**p**) = **q**−**p** = −φ(**p**,**q**)), then this gives (**p**−**q**)^T K (**q**−**p**) = −(**p**−**q**)^T K (**p**−**q**), which is negative unless zero. But MMD² is non-negative. So unless φ(**q**,**p**) is defined as **p**−**q** (symmetric case), the expression gives the wrong sign. Looking back at GPK definition: GPK = φ ◦ (**p**,**q**)^T K φ ◦ (**q**,**p**). For MMD, we need both terms to be **p**−**q** (symmetric mapping) or accept an antisymmetric one. Theorem 1 allows symmetric or antisymmetric φ. But in antisymmetric case, φ(**q**,**p**) = −φ(**p**,**q**), so φ(**q**,**p**) = −(**p**−**q**) = **q**−**p**. Then GPK = (**p**−**q**)^T K (**q**−**p**) = − (**p**−**q**)^T K (**p**−**q**), which is ≤ 0 — contradicts MMD² ≥ 0. So to recover MMD², φ(**p**,**q**) must be symmetric: φ(**p**,**q**) = **p** − **q**, regardless of order. The authors assume this implicitly but don't clarify. The lack of clear specification of how φ treats (**q**,**p**) in the symmetric case undermines the alignment with actual MMD.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of polynomial mapping degree notation in Theorem 2
comments.quote: Then any member of polynomial GPK[ F, φ, p , q ] equipped with polynomial mapping function o φ ( p , q ) =[] [o] l =0  s =0 [α][l,s] [p][l] [q][s]of degree o[∈] [Z] [, has an unbiased plugin-estimator with mapping] function fφ to be:
comments.explanation: The phrase 'polynomial mapping function o φ' appears to be a corrupted OCR rendering. The intended expression is likely 'φ of degree o', given the consistent use of 'o' as the polynomial degree (e.g. in Definition 4). The notation 'o φ' suggests a function named 'oφ', which contradicts prior definitions. However, context resolves this: the summation bounds ∑_{l=0}^o ∑_{s=0}^o match the definition of o as the degree. Thus, this is not a mathematical error in content but a likely OCR misread of 'φ' with a preceding degree marker. Still, the notation 'o φ' could mislead a reader unfamiliar with the surrounding text.

comments.title: Unjustified reuse of constant Z across different corollaries
comments.quote: Where Z is defined in Corollary 3.1
comments.explanation: Corollary 3.3 references 'Z' as defined in Corollary 3.1, but the passage does not show Corollary 3.1 or its definition of Z. From context in Theorem 3 and Corollary 3.2, Z appears to be a normalization constant related to sample sizes and K_max. However, Corollary 3.2 refers to 'τn,m' and states 'with τn,m^(1)= τn and τn,m^(2)= τm', which suggests τ terms (possibly related to convergence) that likely define Z. Without seeing Corollary 3.1, it's unclear whether Z is properly reused or assumed consistent across different asymptotic regimes. While common in asymptotic analysis, the reuse of Z without redefining it creates ambiguity. Given the authors' stated goal of generalizing and tightening bounds, a reader might reasonably expect Z to be context-sensitive. This lacks sufficient justification in the excerpt.

comments.title: Misleading claim about tighter convergence bound compared to Gretton et al.
comments.quote: Interestingly, this bound is the same as the case ρ = 1 in Corollary 3.2. [...] Our bound generalize the bound in (Gretton et al., 2012) and] provide a tighter version.
comments.explanation: The remark claims the authors provide a 'tighter version' of the deviation bound from Gretton et al. (2012), yet explicitly states their bound 'is the same' as the one for ρ=1 in the linear time MMD case. If the bounds are identical, it contradicts the claim of being tighter. One might interpret 'tighter' as 'more generally derived', but the wording 'provide a tighter version' suggests an improvement in the rate or constants, which is not supported here. The context of Theorem 3 and Corollary 3.2 suggests a uniform bound under a more general setup, but no constants are compared. Given that MMD_l already achieves optimal rates, claiming 'tighter' without evidence overstates the contribution. This could mislead readers about theoretical novelty.

comments.title: Missing definition of gρ in power-MMD plugin estimator
comments.quote: with the mapping function fφ ( Si,n_1 , Si,m_1) = gρ ( Ni, n_1) − gρ ( Mi, m_1)
comments.explanation: The function gρ is used in the plugin estimator for power-MMD but is not defined in the provided text. From context (power-MMD defined via φ(p,q) = p^ρ − q^ρ and the use of counts N_i), we expect gρ(N_i, n) to be an unbiased estimator of (p_i)^ρ. In standard Bernstein polynomial methods (e.g., Qian et al., 2011), this would typically be a normalized falling factorial. However, its absence in Definition 8 and Theorem 2 means a reader cannot reproduce the estimator or verify unbiasness. Although likely defined in the appendix, calling this the 'default-plugin-estimator' without specifying fφ makes the method underspecified. Since reproducibility is key in methodology sections (especially for estimators), this omission affects usability.

comments.title: Ambiguity in convergence bound normalization for hypothesis test
comments.quote: Corollary 3.3. A hypothesis test of level α for the null hypothesis p = q has the acceptance region  GPK _E_ [ ~~_√_~~ _FZ,φρ,X,Y_ ]  <  12 [log][(]  _α_ 2  − 1)
comments.explanation: The test statistic involves 'GPK_E' divided by '√Z', with acceptance threshold based on −12 log(2/α). The expression '√Z' suggests Z is a sample-size-dependent scaling factor (e.g., 1/(n1+n2+m1+m2) or similar), necessary for concentration. However, the threshold −12 log(2/α) does not match typical Gaussian or sub-Gaussian tail behavior (which would involve √log(1/α) or similar). For Hoeffding/McDiarmid-type bounds, deviations scale as exp(−2a²Z), so to control type I error at α, one typically sets a proportional to √log(1/α), not log(1/α). A threshold linear in log(1/α) would be excessively conservative and not standard. Given the authors reference McDiarmid in Theorem 3, it is questionable that their bound leads to such a test. The form suggests possible confusion between deviation bounds and p-value calibrations. This could mislead a reader about the test's statistical validity.

comments.title: Incorrectly stated symmetry condition in Theorem 1
comments.quote: 1. symmetric or antisymmetric with respect to p and q : φ ◦ ( p , q ) = φ ◦ ( q , p ) or φ ◦ ( p , q ) = −φ ◦ ( q , p )
comments.explanation: The passage states Theorem 1 requires φ ◦ (p,q) = ±φ ◦ (q,p). However, for GPK_F,φ(p,q) = φ◦(p,q)^T K φ◦(q,p) to be symmetric (a stated assumption), we need φ◦(p,q)^T K φ◦(q,p) = φ◦(q,p)^T K φ◦(p,q), which follows if K is symmetric (true) but does not require φ◦(p,q) to be symmetric or antisymmetric. In fact, for power-MMD with φ(p,q)=p^ρ−q^ρ, φ◦(p,q) = −φ◦(q,p), so antisymmetry aligns with the definition. However, product GPK with φ(p,q)=p^l is not symmetric nor antisymmetric in general. Yet it is not claimed to be a discrepancy kernel. For MMD (case ρ=1), φ(p,q)=p−q is antisymmetric. The condition may thus be intended only for discrepancy kernels (per Definition 3), not for all GPK. Context resolves this: Theorem 1 is specifically for discrepancy probability kernels. Therefore, the condition is correctly applied within its intended scope. No flag.

comments.title: Underspecified convergence bound in Theorem 3
comments.quote: Pr( | GPK _E [ F, φ, X, Y ] − E[GPK _E [ F, φ, X, Y ] ] | ≥ a ) ≤ 2 e[−] [2] Z[a] [2]
comments.explanation: The bound lacks units: Z must depend on sample sizes and K to make the exponent dimensionless. From Corollary 3.2, we see terms involving n and m, suggesting Z scales as 1/n or similar. However, Theorem 3 presents Z without definition. While later corollaries use τ terms, assuming Z is an inverse sample-size quantity, its omission breaks reproducibility. The McDiarmid-type bound should have a scaling involving max influence (which depends on K_max and degree o), but here it is collapsed into Z. This is common in high-level presentations, but since the paper claims analyzable bounds and comparisons, stating it non-constructively undermines rigor. Given that Z is later said to be defined in Corollary 3.1 (not shown), this may be a forward reference, but it leaves the theorem underspecified in isolation.

comments.title: Incorrect deviation bound in Corollary 3.2 due to missing factor in exponent
comments.explanation: The stated deviation bound in Corollary 3.2 appears to be incorrect because the exponent in the exponential bound contains a factor of 8 in the denominator that does not follow from Theorem 3. According to Theorem 3, the bound is 2 exp(−2Z a² / K_max²), where Z = 1/n₁ + 1/n₂ + 1/m₁ + 1/m₂. When all sample sizes are n, Z = 4/n. Substituting into Theorem 3 yields 2 exp(−2*(4/n)*a² / K_max²) = 2 exp(−8a² / (n K_max²)). However, the corollary states the bound as 2 exp(−2a² / (8K_max² n⁻¹)) = 2 exp(−(2n a²)/(8K_max²)) = 2 exp(−(n a²)/(4K_max²)), which is asymptotically looser and differs by a factor of 32 in the exponent. This is not an OCR error because the coefficient mismatch persists under the correct substitutions. The direction of the inequality is preserved, but the bound is significantly weaker than what Theorem 3 implies. Given that the paper claims to tighten the deviation bound relative to Gretton et al. (2012), this error undermines the stated advantage.

comments.title: Incorrect test statistic in Corollary 3.3 with ill-defined \sqrt{FZ}
comments.quote: GPK_E[ \sqrt{FZ}, φρ, X, Y ]
comments.explanation: The notation \sqrt{FZ} in Corollary 3.3 is undefined and appears to be a substantial error. Earlier in the paper, F is defined as a unit ball in an RKHS, not a scalar or matrix, so taking its square root makes no sense. Similarly, Z is a scalar (sum of reciprocals of sample sizes), but FZ as a product is not defined. The standard linear MMD test statistic involves scaling by √n, not a function of F and Z. Given that Corollary 3.3 is intended to state a hypothesis test, and the acceptance region depends on this expression, its lack of definition renders the test procedure ambiguous. While one might interpret this as an OCR error where \sqrt{n} was misread as \sqrt{FZ}, the recurrence of 'FZ' in the same form in the test description suggests it is not a simple typo. Even if FZ were meant to be Z, the square root scaling is inconsistent with the deviation bounds, which suggest a scaling by √Z or √n.

comments.title: Overstatement of power-MMD performance for ρ = 1 case
comments.quote: we provide a tighter bound for the test statistic, thus we will certainly have a better performance using power-MMD
comments.explanation: The authors claim that because their deviation bound is tighter (in the exponent), the power-MMD test 'certainly' has better performance than the MMD_l test of Gretton et al. (2012). However, a tighter deviation bound does not necessarily imply better test performance (i.e. higher power under alternatives). Test performance depends on both the variance of the estimator under the null and the expected value under the alternative. While a tighter concentration bound may allow for smaller critical values or lower Type I error control, it does not by itself guarantee higher detection power. The authors correctly derive a bound, but overinterpret its implications. A more accurate statement would be that their analysis provides stronger theoretical guarantees, not that performance is certainly better.

comments.title: Numerical stabilization in Corollary 3.4 uses inconsistent normalization factor
comments.quote: GPK_E'[\cdot] = \sum_{i=1}^k \left( \frac{g_\rho(N_{i,n_1}) - g_\rho(M_{i,m_1})}{(S_N / n)^\rho} \right) K_{ii} \left( \frac{g_\rho(N_{i,n_2}) - g_\rho(M_{i,m_2})}{(S_N / n)^\rho} \right)
comments.explanation: In Corollary 3.4, the normalization factor (S_N / n)^ρ is used, where S_N is the maximum count across all samples and n is the per-split sample size. However, S_N / n is a fraction ≤ 1, so (S_N / n)^ρ becomes very small for large ρ, which could actually amplify numerical errors rather than reduce them when dividing. The intended purpose is to prevent g_ρ(N_i) from being too small, but g_ρ(N_i) is already estimated from counts and likely scales with (N_i/n)^ρ. A more natural normalization would be by (N/n)^ρ for some global N, or by S_N^ρ, not (S_N/n)^ρ. For example, if g_ρ(N) ≈ (N/n)^ρ, then dividing by (S_N/n)^ρ gives (N/S_N)^ρ, which is stable, so this may be the intended behavior. However, the current form introduces a spurious factor of n^ρ in the denominator, making the statistic scale inversely with n^ρ, which is not consistent with expected behavior of MMD-type statistics. This suggests either a missing n^ρ factor elsewhere or a notational confusion.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of superscript l for linear-time MMD
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, the linear-time MMD statistic is referred to as MMD_l, but in several places—including Remark 3.1 and 5.3.1—it is typeset as MMD[2] _l_ with a superscript 2. This appears to be an OCR artifact where _l_ (subscript lowercase L) was misread as [2] _l_. The MMD² notation usually means the squared MMD, which is not the same as the linear-time estimator. While context suggests the authors mean MMD_l, the repeated occurrence of 'MMD[2]' instead of 'MMD' before the _l subscript raises ambiguity. Given that the paper compares its method to MMD_l, correctly identifying the baseline is crucial. This is likely a consistent OCR error rather than an author error, but it affects readability and precision. Since the meaning is recoverable from context, it doesn’t rise to a major issue.

comments.title: Undefined mapping function fφ in Definition 7 and Theorem 2
comments.quote: where fφ is a function related to function φ, and K is the gram matrix brought by F.
comments.explanation: In Definition 7 and Theorem 2, the mapping function fφ is introduced but never formally defined—only described as 'related to φ'. The connection is clarified later (e.g. for power-MMD, fφ(S_i,n1, S_i,m1) = gρ(N_i,n1) − gρ(M_i,m1)), but this dependency on count-based estimators should be defined when fφ is first introduced. Readers cannot verify the unbiasedness claim without knowing how fφ depends on φ. The appendix references g_j(k,n) but does not define it. While the idea is that fφ implements the Bernstein polynomial correction for the plugin estimator, this critical mechanism is underspecified. This is especially problematic for reproducibility: one cannot implement the estimator without knowing how fφ is constructed from φ.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) that bridges discrete distribution estimation and sample-based hypothesis testing by formalizing kernels over distributions rather than raw data points. The core contribution—applying Bernstein polynomial techniques to analyze the bias and convergence of plugin estimators within an RKHS framework—is theoretically compelling and addresses a relevant gap in high-dimensional discrete setting analysis. However, the manuscript suffers from significant clarity and presentation issues that impede its accessibility. The notation, while intended to be rigorous, is frequently overloaded (e.g., the repetitive and inconsistent use of $\phi \circ$ and bracketed indices), and the mathematical definitions are hampered by distracting typos and erratic formatting that suggest a lack of final-stage proofreading. Furthermore, the motivation for the GPK framework remains somewhat abstract; to strengthen the paper, the authors must clarify how the increased generality of the GPK provides a distinct advantage over standard MMD in practical two-sample testing scenarios, rather than just serving as a repackaging of existing results. A more precise explanation of the "power-MMD" extension and its comparative computational performance would greatly improve the paper’s impact.

comments.title: Ambiguous and Inconsistent Definition of Mapping Function
comments.quote: GPK_{F,φ}(p,q) = φ ◦ (p,q) K φ ◦ (q,p) ...
comments.explanation: The notation for the mapping function φ and the composition operator '◦' is used inconsistently throughout the text (e.g., as a vector-to-vector map, a scalar-to-scalar map, and a polynomial definition). Definition 4 and the associated polynomial GPK formulation suffer from missing indexing (e.g., specifying (φ(p, q))_i) and garbled transcription. This lack of formal precision makes the GPK framework difficult to interpret and reproduce.

comments.title: Mathematical Error in Proof of Theorem 1
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, v K v[T] ≥ 0, where equality holds if and only if v = 0
comments.explanation: The proof erroneously equates positive semidefinite (semipositive definite) matrices with positive definite matrices. The claim that v K v^T = 0 implies v = 0 is false for general PSD matrices, which may have a non-trivial null space. This is a significant logical error in the derivation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Claim regarding Power-MMD Discrepancy
comments.quote: φ ( p , q ) = p[ρ] − q[ρ]
comments.explanation: The authors define a discrepancy kernel based on p^ρ - q^ρ, but provide no proof for the claimed condition that the kernel vanishes if and only if p=q. For general ρ > 1 and probability distributions, this mapping is not necessarily injective or sufficient to distinguish distributions, rendering the claim of a 'discrepancy kernel' mathematically unsupported.

comments.title: Missing Functional Form for Unbiased Plugin Estimator
comments.quote: fφ to be:
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 asserts the existence of an unbiased plugin-estimator and references a function fφ, but fails to provide its explicit functional form. Consequently, the core algorithmic contribution regarding unbiased estimation is underspecified and non-reproducible.

comments.title: Inconsistent or Incorrect Deviation Bound Transcription
comments.quote: Pr( | GPK E [ F, φ, X, Y ] − E[GPK E [ F, φ, X, Y ]] | ≥ a ) ≤ 2 e[−][2] Z[a][2]
comments.explanation: The deviation bound provided for Theorem 3 lacks the expected dependency on sample size (n). As written, it implies a concentration result independent of sample size, which is inconsistent with standard concentration inequalities (e.g., McDiarmid's) usually applied to such estimators.

comments.title: Mathematical Inconsistency in Acceptance Region Condition
comments.explanation: The mathematical expression defining the acceptance region for the hypothesis test is illegible due to corruption. As this threshold is essential for the practical implementation of the paper's proposed test, its omission or corruption constitutes a critical barrier to the validity of the results.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) that bridges discrete distribution estimation and sample-based hypothesis testing by formalizing kernels over distributions rather than raw data points. The core contribution—applying Bernstein polynomial techniques to analyze the bias and convergence of plugin estimators within an RKHS framework—is theoretically compelling and addresses a relevant gap in high-dimensional discrete setting analysis. However, the manuscript suffers from significant clarity and presentation issues that impede its accessibility. The notation, while intended to be rigorous, is frequently overloaded (e.g., the repetitive and inconsistent use of $\phi \circ$ and bracketed indices), and the mathematical definitions are hampered by distracting typos and erratic formatting that suggest a lack of final-stage proofreading. Furthermore, the motivation for the GPK framework remains somewhat abstract; to strengthen the paper, the authors must clarify how the increased generality of the GPK provides a distinct advantage over standard MMD in practical two-sample testing scenarios, rather than just serving as a repackaging of existing results. A more precise explanation of the "power-MMD" extension and its comparative computational performance would greatly improve the paper’s impact.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in GPK Definition
comments.explanation: The formula for the Gram matrix multiplication appears to have an index mismatch. In the matrix-vector representation φ(p,q)^T K φ(q,p), if φ(p,q) is a column vector, then the result is a scalar. In the summation notation, it is written as index i and j. However, the expression φ(p,q)_i K_{ij} φ(q,p)_j implies a quadratic form similar to the standard RKHS MMD, but the term φ(q,p)_j suggests that the entry at index j of the mapping function should be used. The notation [φ][ ◦][(] [qj, pj][)] is ambiguous; if it follows the definition of φ ◦ (q,p) as a vector, the j-th component is (φ ◦ (q,p))_j. While likely an OCR-derived notation error, the mix of vector-matrix notation and index notation is inconsistent.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of Mapping Function
comments.quote: φ ◦ (p, q) is an element-wise mapping function on discrete distribution p, q ∈S, which maps from R [k] × R [k] to R [k]
comments.explanation: The symbol ◦ is used in Definition 2 both as an operator notation for element-wise mapping and as a symbol inside the function definition. In Section 2, the authors define φ ◦ (p,q) to denote an element-wise function, but then write (φ ◦ (p,q))_i = φ ◦ (pi, qi). This suggests φ is the function, and ◦ is a placeholder or operator. However, in Definition 4, the authors clarify the mapping function is a polynomial of p and q. The notation remains conflated throughout the text, potentially leading a reader to confuse the function 'φ' with the output of the '◦' operator.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in Definition 4
comments.explanation: The notation p[l] is introduced in Section 2 as an element-wise function (powering the components of p). However, in the context of the polynomial GPK, the standard interpretation would be the l-th power of the vector p (or components). Given that section 5.1 and 5.2 describe power-MMD where ρ is an exponent, using [l] as a superscript/index is confusing. Furthermore, the definition of the polynomial mapping is missing the explicit support index 'i', which is necessary since the formula maps R[k] x R[k] to R[k].

comments.title: Inconsistency in Theorem 1 Proof regarding Gram Matrix property
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, v K v[T] ≥ 0, where equality holds if and only if v = 0
comments.explanation: The proof claims that for a semipositive definite matrix K, v K v^T = 0 if and only if v = 0. This is technically incorrect; for a positive semidefinite (semipositive definite) matrix, v K v^T = 0 can hold for non-zero vectors v that lie in the null space of K. The condition given later in Theorem 1 (property 2) is meant to guarantee the identity, but stating this property as a general definition of semipositive definite matrices is mathematically misleading for the reader.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Incomplete definition of Polynomial GPK
comments.explanation: The OCR rendering here is significantly garbled, but even projecting the intended meaning, the definition of 'polynomial in p and q' is underspecified regarding the dimensions. Since p and q are vectors in R^[k], it is unclear if the mapping is applied element-wise (scalar polynomials for each entry i) or if there is a cross-entry interaction. Given the surrounding text, it is likely element-wise, but the formal definition lacks the necessary indexing clarification (e.g., specifying (φ(p, q))_i = Σ_{l,s} α_{l,s} p_i^l q_i^s).

comments.title: Potential contradiction in Power-MMD definition
comments.quote: α[ρ], 0 = 1 , α 0,[ρ] = − 1 , and all other coefficients are 0 . the corresponding mapping function is: φ ( p , q ) = p[ρ] − q[ρ]
comments.explanation: In Definition 6, the mapping is stated as φ(p, q) = p^ρ - q^ρ. However, the requirement for a discrepancy kernel (Theorem 1) usually necessitates that the mapping captures the difference in such a way that the kernel vanishes iff p=q. If p and q are probability distributions (non-negative and summing to 1), p^ρ - q^ρ = 0 does not necessarily imply p=q for general ρ > 1 (e.g., consider different distributions on a support with two elements where specific values of ρ collapse the mapping). The paper claims this is a discrepancy kernel, but the implication (p^ρ = q^ρ ⟹ p=q) is only true for ρ odd or specific conditions not fully articulated.

comments.title: Undefined indexing variable in Definition of GPK
comments.quote: GPK_F, φ( p, q ) = (φ ◦ (p, q))^T K (φ ◦ (q, p)) = Σ_{i∈[k]} Σ_{j∈[k]} φ ◦ (p_i, q_i) K_{ij} φ ◦ (q_j, p_j)
comments.explanation: The notation φ ◦ (p_i, q_i) is used here, but previously defined as the i-th element of a mapping function. If φ ◦ (p, q) is a vector-valued function, the notation should be consistent between the vector expression and the Einstein/summation component. The distinction between the mapping function acting on the vector vs. the scalers p_i, q_i is slightly muddied, and the notation φ ◦ (p, q) is used somewhat interchangeably as a vector-to-vector map and a scalar-to-scalar map.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the definition of the Acceptance Region
comments.explanation: The expression within the acceptance region condition is mathematically nonsensical due to the symbols used, likely resulting from corrupted OCR text (e.g., ' 12' and ' α 2  − 1'). While I understand the intent is to provide a threshold based on a significance level alpha, the actual functional form is illegible. This prevents a reader from understanding how the hypothesis test is performed, which is a core claim of the paper.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the mapping function in Theorem 2
comments.quote: fφ to be:
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 states that any member of the polynomial GPK family has an unbiased plugin-estimator and references a mapping function fφ. However, after the colon, the text fails to provide the explicit functional form for fφ, leaving the definition of the unbiased estimator underspecified and non-reproducible for the reader.

comments.title: Mismatched index in deviation bound
comments.explanation: The deviation bound in Theorem 3 is presented as '2 e[−][2] Z[a][2]'. In concentration inequality literature (like McDiarmid's), the exponent typically involves a dependency on the sample size (n) and the threshold (a). The current notation suggests a constant independent of sample size, which contradicts the standard convergence behavior of plugin-estimators. This appears to be a significant error in the transcription of the bound.

comments.title: Unjustified claim regarding tighter bounds
comments.quote: Our bound generalize the bound in (Gretton et al., 2012) and provide a tighter version.
comments.explanation: The authors claim their bound for power-MMD (with rho=1) is 'tighter' than the existing linear time MMD bound from Gretton et al. (2012). However, they provide no rigorous analytical proof comparing the constants or decay rates. Given that the result is simply stated as 'interestingly, this bound is the same', the assertion of it being 'tighter' is an unsubstantiated claim.

comments.title: Possible Typo/OCR Error in Citation Name
comments.quote: However, bais reduction techniqes proposed in (Yi et al., 2018) and (Yi & Alon, 2020) could be used,
comments.explanation: In Section 1 and in the References, the authors correctly list the author 'Hao Yi and Orlitsky Alon' (though often reversed in formatting). However, in the summary, the word 'bais' is a clear typo for 'bias'. While I am instructed to be lenient with typos, this occurs in the summary section and seems to stem from a slight OCR or manual drafting slip relative to the consistent phrasing elsewhere. It is a minor linguistic issue rather than a mathematical one, but it detracts from the paper's academic presentation in the concluding summary.

comments.title: Oversimplification of Unbiased Estimator Availability
comments.quote: For members of GPK which do not belong to polynomial GPK, it is not easy to design an unbiased estimators.
comments.explanation: The authors state that for non-polynomial GPK, it is 'not easy' to design unbiased estimators. Given that the paper focuses on the polynomial GPK family specifically because Bernstein polynomials allow for tractable unbiased estimation, this framing implies that such estimators either don't exist for other families or are categorically intractable. A more precise claim would clarify that this property is a specific advantage of the polynomial GPK family due to the linearity of the expectation of the Bernstein basis, rather than suggesting a general difficulty gap for the entire GPK framework.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper introduces the Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK), a framework defining kernels over discrete distributions via element-wise mappings and a fixed Gram matrix on the support, and proposes its use for two-sample testing with a focus on bias analysis of plugin estimators. The topic is relevant to the intersection of kernel methods and discrete distribution estimation, and the connection to bias reduction via Bernstein polynomial techniques suggests a potentially valuable technical contribution, particularly if it leads to tighter bounds or superior test statistics like the proposed "power-MMD." However, the submission suffers from significant clarity and presentation issues that currently hinder evaluation. Notably, the notation is inconsistent and non-standard (e.g., using $R_{[k]}$ instead of $\mathbb{R}^k$, ambiguous vector/matrix multiplication in Definition 2, and undefined symbols like $\mathbf{p}^{[\rho]}$), and the prose contains informal phrasing ("people focus on," "not so much work") unsuitable for a top-tier venue. Furthermore, the relationship to existing literature requires sharper articulation; the claim that GPK generalizes MMD and probability product kernels needs precise mathematical verification to ensure these are genuine generalizations rather than reformulations of known structures, and the distinction between kernel definitions and discrepancy metrics must be clarified. The paper would benefit from a rigorous overhaul of notation, a more formal presentation of definitions (including the truncated Definition 3), and a clearer statement of how the proposed bias analysis improves upon or differs from standard MMD estimators.

comments.title: Invalid threshold in Corollary 3.3 acceptance region
comments.explanation: The mathematical core `log(α/2 - 1)` is critically flawed. For any standard significance level α ∈ (0, 1), the argument `α/2 - 1` is negative, rendering the natural logarithm undefined over the reals. Following the authors' stated use of McDiarmid's inequality, the threshold should logically derive from inverting `2 exp(-2a^2/Z) ≤ α`, which yields a bound proportional to `√(Z log(2/α)/2)`. As written, the acceptance region is mathematically invalid, and the two-sample test procedure cannot be implemented.

comments.quote: 2 e[−]_[2] _Z[a]_[2]_ ... where τn_1 is a constant related to sample size n_1, τm_1 is a constant related to sample size m_1, the same follows for τn_2 and τm_2
comments.explanation: The convergence bound `2 exp(-2a^2/Z)` in Theorem 3 and the bounded-difference constants τ_{n1}, τ_{m1}, etc. in Lemma 4 are never precisely defined. In applications of McDiarmid's inequality, the denominator corresponds to the sum of squared bounded-difference constants, which must be explicitly tied to problem parameters (e.g., support size k, polynomial degree o, max Gram matrix entry K_max, sample sizes). Their vague or missing definitions make it impossible to verify the tightness of the concentration bounds in Corollary 3.2, reproduce the theoretical analysis, or use the results for practical sample size planning.

comments.title: Contradictory symmetry condition in Theorem 1
comments.quote: 1. symmetric or antisymmetric with respect to p and q : φ◦(p,q) = φ◦(q,p) or φ◦(p,q) = −φ◦(q,p)
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 allows the mapping function φ to be either symmetric or antisymmetric. While symmetric φ trivially yields a non-negative GPK (as vKv^T ≥ 0 for PSD K), an antisymmetric φ (φ(p,q) = -φ(q,p)) would substitute into the definition as φ(p,q)K(-φ(p,q))^T = -φ(p,q)Kφ(p,q)^T ≤ 0. Discrepancy measures and kernels are fundamentally expected to be non-negative and vanish only when distributions match. The authors likely meant to allow element-wise antisymmetry (e.g., p_i - q_i) while preserving the overall non-negativity of the quadratic form, or intended to restrict to symmetric φ. As phrased, the antisymmetric case violates the core properties of a discrepancy kernel.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a generalized probability kernel (GPK) for discrete distributions, aiming to unify existing discrepancy measures like MMD and probability product kernels while connecting discrete distribution estimation with kernel-based hypothesis testing. While the core idea is potentially interesting, the exposition suffers from significant clarity issues that undermine its quality. The text contains numerous typographical errors (e.g., “DEFINATION,” “discreteness” as a section heading) and incomplete sentences, and the mathematical notation is often ambiguous (e.g., inconsistent use of “◦” and square brackets). The motivation for the GPK framework is not clearly articulated—the authors state that viewing discrepancy measures as probability kernels is beneficial for plugin estimation, but they fail to concretely illustrate why this perspective yields new insights beyond existing work. Moreover, the proposed “power-MMD” is mentioned in the abstract but not defined or motivated in the provided excerpt, leaving the reader without a sense of the paper’s main technical contribution. The most significant issues are the lack of rigorous definitions (e.g., the mapping function φ is introduced but its role and properties are left vague), the absence of concrete examples showing how GPK generalizes or improves upon prior methods, and the overall poor presentation quality, which detracts from the paper’s credibility and makes it difficult to assess its scientific merit.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of degree in polynomial GPK
comments.quote: \varphi \circ (\mathbf{p},\mathbf{q}) =[]_{l=0}^o _{s=0} \alpha_{l,s} \mathbf{p}^{l} \mathbf{q}^{s} \text{ where } o \in \mathbb{Z} \text{ is the degree of the polynomial}
comments.explanation: The degree 'o' is used, but is this the maximum total degree (l+s) or maximum individual exponent? For a bivariate polynomial ∑ α_{l,s} p^l q^s, the degree is usually max(l+s). The notation 'o' is ambiguous. Later in Example 3.1.2 (product GPK), they use exponent 'l' which is consistent with the definition if l is maximum exponent for p. But the sum over s from 0 to 'o' suggests each term has s ≤ o, which is redundant if degree is max(l+s). The definition is underspecified: 'o' could be the maximum of l or s individually, or max(l+s). This ambiguity could mislead a reader implementing the family.

comments.title: Overclaim: 'any member of polynomial GPK has an unbiased plugin-estimator' without explicit existence proof
comments.quote: Theorem 2. ... Then any member of polynomial GPK[F, φ, p, q] equipped with polynomial mapping function ... has an unbiased plugin-estimator with mapping function f_φ to be: ...
comments.explanation: The theorem statement claims existence of unbiased estimator, but the provided expression for f_φ is not fully specified in the passage (the series/coefficients are cut off by OCR). Moreover, the proof is deferred to appendix. Given the paper's context, the core idea (Bernstein polynomials) is standard, but the claim 'any member' requires that the polynomial coefficients are all non-negative or that the estimator is constructed via linear combination of products of binomial moments. The text does not clarify if the mapping φ may include negative coefficients (e.g., MMD has negative coefficients). This is non-trivial: unbiased estimation of p^l q^s requires separate independent samples for p and q, and the paper's four-sample split achieves this, but the theorem's condition on α_{l,s} is not stated. Without the appendix, the justification is insufficient.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement: 'any discrepancy measure of distribution p and q could potentially be valid probability kernels, not so much work focuses on this'
comments.quote: As is easily seen, any discrepancy measure of distribution p and q could potentially be valid probability kernels, not so much work focuses on this.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'could potentially be valid probability kernels' is ambiguous. A kernel must be positive definite. Discrepancy measures (e.g., total variation distance) are usually metrics, not necessarily positive definite kernels. The sentence overstates by implying all discrepancy measures are kernels. Context later clarifies they focus on GPK which is a specific quadratic form, but the introductory prose is misleading.

comments.title: Underspecified: definition of g_ρ function

It is presumably the Bernstein polynomial basis function (as in Qian et al. 2011), but the reader cannot reproduce the estimator without knowing whether g_ρ(N,n) = N^ρ / n^ρ or the unbiased version g_ρ(N,n) = n^{-ρ} N(N-1)...(N-ρ+1) (falling factorial). The latter is standard for unbiased estimation of p^ρ, but the text does not specify. This is a key implementation detail.

comments.quote: Pr(|GPK_E[F, φ_ρ, X, Y] - E[GPK_E[F, φ_ρ, X, Y]]| ≥ a) ≤ 2 exp(-2 Z a^2) ... where Z = ...
comments.explanation: The bound uses exp(-2 Z a^2). For McDiarmid's inequality with sum of squared differences, the exponent is typically -2 a^2 / (∑ c_i^2). Here Z is defined as a complex expression involving (2ρ K_max / (n1)) etc. Without checking the proof in appendix, it's unclear if the factor of 2 in the exponent is correct (McDiarmid: Pr(|X - EX| ≥ ε) ≤ 2 exp(-2 ε^2 / ∑ c_i^2)). The subsequent Corollary 3.2 uses exp(-n a^2 / (8 ρ^2 K_max^2)), which seems plausible. However, the intermediate expression '2 Z a^2' with Z in denominator is prone to algebraic error. Flagging as potential issue if not carefully derived.

comments.title: Ambiguous: 'acceptance region' for hypothesis test uses absolute value of estimator
comments.explanation: The notation is garbled: '√ FZ' seems to be an OCR artifact (likely meant √(Z) or denominator). The condition should be |GPK_E| < threshold. The acceptance region as written is unparseable. Also, the test as described compares absolute value to a threshold, but for a two-sample test with null hypothesis p=q, the population GPK is 0, and the test should reject for large |GPK_E|. The threshold uses log(α/2)^{-1} inside a square root—this would yield a negative number inside sqrt if α/2 < 1, which is always true. Likely meant √( (1/2) log(2/α) ). This is a serious presentation error.

comments.title: Proof of Theorem 1 incorrectly assumes positive definiteness of the Gram matrix
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, v K v^T >= 0, where equality holds if and only if v = 0
comments.explanation: The proof claims that for a semipositive definite matrix K, the quadratic form v^T K v is zero if and only if v = 0. This property holds only for positive definite matrices, not for all semipositive definite matrices. A semipositive (positive semidefinite) matrix can have non-zero vectors in its nullspace, for which v^T K v = 0. The Gram matrix K is not guaranteed to be positive definite unless the kernel RK is strictly positive definite and the support points are distinct; the paper does not state this assumption. Additionally, the proof implicitly treats GPK as v^T K v (or v K v^T) but does not handle the antisymmetric mapping case, where GPK = -v K v^T. While the condition for a discrepancy kernel (zero iff p=q) may still hold, the reasoning that v^T K v ≥ 0 with equality only at v=0 is insufficiently justified for antisymmetric φ and semipositive K. This undermines the validity of Theorem 1 as presented.

comments.quote: GPK_{F,\varphi}(\mathbf{p},\mathbf{q}) = \varphi \circ (\mathbf{p},\mathbf{q}) K \varphi \circ (\mathbf{q},\mathbf{p})^{[T]} =[]_{i\in[k]} _{j\in[k]} [\varphi][\circ][(][p_i, q_i][)] [K_{ij}][\varphi][\circ][(][q_j, p_j][)]
comments.explanation: The equation is garbled by OCR artifacts and missing parentheses. The intended expression should be a quadratic form: (φ∘(p,q))^T K (φ∘(q,p)). The OCR produced bracketed '[T]' superscript and placeholder symbols. While the meaning is clear from context, the formula as shown is technically unreadable.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of degree in polynomial GPK
comments.quote: \varphi \circ (\mathbf{p},\mathbf{q}) =[]_{l=0}^o _{s=0} \alpha_{l,s} \mathbf{p}^{l} \mathbf{q}^{s} \text{ where } o \in \mathbb{Z} \text{ is the degree of the polynomial}
comments.explanation: The degree 'o' is used, but is this the maximum total degree (l+s) or maximum individual exponent? For a bivariate polynomial ∑ α_{l,s} p^l q^s, the degree is usually max(l+s). The notation 'o' is ambiguous. Later in Example 3.1.2 (product GPK), they use exponent 'l' which is consistent with the definition if l is maximum exponent for p. But the sum over s from 0 to 'o' suggests each term has s ≤ o, which is redundant if degree is max(l+s). The definition is underspecified: 'o' could be the maximum of l or s individually, or max(l+s). This ambiguity could mislead a reader implementing the family.

comments.title: Overclaim: 'any member of polynomial GPK has an unbiased plugin-estimator' without explicit existence proof
comments.quote: Theorem 2. ... Then any member of polynomial GPK[F, φ, p, q] equipped with polynomial mapping function ... has an unbiased plugin-estimator with mapping function f_φ to be: ...
comments.explanation: The theorem statement claims existence of unbiased estimator, but the provided expression for f_φ is not fully specified in the passage (the series/coefficients are cut off by OCR). Moreover, the proof is deferred to appendix. Given the paper's context, the core idea (Bernstein polynomials) is standard, but the claim 'any member' requires that the polynomial coefficients are all non-negative or that the estimator is constructed via linear combination of products of binomial moments. The text does not clarify if the mapping φ may include negative coefficients (e.g., MMD has negative coefficients). This is non-trivial: unbiased estimation of p^l q^s requires separate independent samples for p and q, and the paper's four-sample split achieves this, but the theorem's condition on α_{l,s} is not stated. Without the appendix, the justification is insufficient.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement: 'any discrepancy measure of distribution p and q could potentially be valid probability kernels, not so much work focuses on this'
comments.quote: As is easily seen, any discrepancy measure of distribution p and q could potentially be valid probability kernels, not so much work focuses on this.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'could potentially be valid probability kernels' is ambiguous. A kernel must be positive definite. Discrepancy measures (e.g., total variation distance) are usually metrics, not necessarily positive definite kernels. The sentence overstates by implying all discrepancy measures are kernels. Context later clarifies they focus on GPK which is a specific quadratic form, but the introductory prose is misleading.

comments.title: Underspecified: definition of g_ρ function
comments.explanation: The function g_ρ is referenced but never defined in the passage. It is presumably the Bernstein polynomial basis function (as in Qian et al. 2011), but the reader cannot reproduce the estimator without knowing whether g_ρ(N,n) = N^ρ / n^ρ or the unbiased version g_ρ(N,n) = n^{-ρ} N(N-1)...(N-ρ+1) (falling factorial). The latter is standard for unbiased estimation of p^ρ, but the text does not specify. This is a key implementation detail.

comments.title: Potentially missing factor in deviation bound for power-MMD
comments.quote: Pr(|GPK_E[F, φ_ρ, X, Y] - E[GPK_E[F, φ_ρ, X, Y]]| ≥ a) ≤ 2 exp(-2 Z a^2) ... where Z = ...
comments.explanation: The bound uses exp(-2 Z a^2). For McDiarmid's inequality with sum of squared differences, the exponent is typically -2 a^2 / (∑ c_i^2). Here Z is defined as a complex expression involving (2ρ K_max / (n1)) etc. Without checking the proof in appendix, it's unclear if the factor of 2 in the exponent is correct (McDiarmid: Pr(|X - EX| ≥ ε) ≤ 2 exp(-2 ε^2 / ∑ c_i^2)). The subsequent Corollary 3.2 uses exp(-n a^2 / (8 ρ^2 K_max^2)), which seems plausible. However, the intermediate expression '2 Z a^2' with Z in denominator is prone to algebraic error. Flagging as potential issue if not carefully derived.

comments.title: Ambiguous: 'acceptance region' for hypothesis test uses absolute value of estimator
comments.explanation: The notation is garbled: '√ FZ' seems to be an OCR artifact (likely meant √(Z) or denominator). The condition should be |GPK_E| < threshold. The acceptance region as written is unparseable. Also, the test as described compares absolute value to a threshold, but for a two-sample test with null hypothesis p=q, the population GPK is 0, and the test should reject for large |GPK_E|. The threshold uses log(α/2)^{-1} inside a square root—this would yield a negative number inside sqrt if α/2 < 1, which is always true. Likely meant √( (1/2) log(2/α) ). This is a serious presentation error.

comments.title: Proof of Theorem 1 incorrectly assumes positive definiteness of the Gram matrix
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, v K v^T >= 0, where equality holds if and only if v = 0
comments.explanation: The proof claims that for a semipositive definite matrix K, the quadratic form v^T K v is zero if and only if v = 0. This property holds only for positive definite matrices, not for all semipositive definite matrices. A semipositive (positive semidefinite) matrix can have non-zero vectors in its nullspace, for which v^T K v = 0. The Gram matrix K is not guaranteed to be positive definite unless the kernel RK is strictly positive definite and the support points are distinct; the paper does not state this assumption. Additionally, the proof implicitly treats GPK as v^T K v (or v K v^T) but does not handle the antisymmetric mapping case, where GPK = -v K v^T. While the condition for a discrepancy kernel (zero iff p=q) may still hold, the reasoning that v^T K v ≥ 0 with equality only at v=0 is insufficiently justified for antisymmetric φ and semipositive K. This undermines the validity of Theorem 1 as presented.

comments.title: Theorem 2 missing the explicit definition of f_φ
comments.quote: _**Theorem 2.** _Denote_ _Then any member of polynomial_ GPK[ _F, φ,_ _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ] _equipped with polynomial mapping function o φ_ ( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) =[] _[o] l_ =0  _s_ =0 _[α][l,s]_ _**[p]**[l]_ _**[q]**[s][of degree][ o][∈]_[Z] _[, has an unbiased plugin-estimator with mapping] function fφ to be:_
comments.explanation: The theorem statement breaks off after 'fφ to be:' without providing the actual formula for the unbiased plugin-estimator. The remainder of the sentence is missing, presumably due to OCR loss. The claim of existence of an unbiased estimator is central to the paper, but the definition needed to verify or apply the estimator is absent from the passage. Even assuming the most plausible intended symbols, the reader cannot recover the estimator from what is given.

comments.title: Denominator Z in Theorem 3 is not specified
comments.quote:
has the convergence bound: _∀a >_ 0 _,_ Pr( _|_ GPK _E_ [ _F, φ, X, Y_ ] _−_ E[GPK _E_ [ _F, φ, X, Y_ ]] _| ≥ a_ ) _≤_ 2 _e[−]_[2] _Z[a]_[2] where 

_Kmax is the largest value of entries in K_
comments.explanation: The bound involves a quantity Z, but its definition is completely omitted in the passage. Only K_max is mentioned, but Z likely depends on more than that (e.g., sample sizes, polynomial degree, coefficients). Without Z, the bound is not interpretable and cannot be used for hypothesis testing or comparison. An expression for Z may appear elsewhere in the paper (e.g., in the appendix), but the passage as presented is missing a critical component of the theorem.

comments.title: Cross-reference to section 4.1.3 instead of 3.1.3
comments.quote: As we already discussed in section 4.1.3, power-MMD is a subset of polynomial GPK.
comments.explanation: The power-MMD was defined in section 3.1.3 of the paper (Example 3), not in section 4.1.3. There is no section 4.1.3 visible in the passage; section 4.1 deals with polynomial GPK and unbiased estimators, not the definition of power-MMD. This is a minor inconsistency that could confuse a reader trying to locate the original definition.

comments.title: Unsupported claims of equivalence to MMD^2_l and tighter bound
comments.quote: Remark 3.1. ... the default-plugin-estimator of this case is equivalent to the linear time statistic MMD^2_l proposed in (Gretton et al., 2012): GPK_E[F, φl, X, Y] = MMD^2_l[F, X, Y] ... Remark 3.2. ... Our bound generalize the bound in (Gretton et al., 2012) and provide a tighter version.
comments.explanation: The passage asserts equivalence between the default plugin-estimator for ρ=1 and the linear-time MMD estimator, and later claims the derived deviation bound generalizes and tightens the bound from Gretton et al. (2012). Neither claim is justified or even sketched in the passage; the derivations are deferred to the appendix. Without supporting reasoning or explicit formulas for the compared bounds, the reader cannot assess the validity of these strong claims. The paper's contributions hinge on these comparisons, making the lack of support a significant gap.

comments.title: Garbled acceptance region formula in Corollary 3.3
comments.explanation: The formula for the acceptance region is heavily corrupted by OCR artifacts. The intended expression is not recoverable with confidence; for instance, a standard Hoeffding-based bound would involve √(Z/2·log(2/α)), but the garbled text includes a square root, a fraction '12', and '−1', which do not match any common form. If this corrupted formula were used in a two-sample test, it would lead to incorrect critical values. The text is ambiguous and likely mathematically wrong as presented.

comments.title: Undefined quantity GPK'_E in Corollary 3.4
comments.quote: For cases when _Ni_ are not far less from _SN_ , GPK _[′] E_[will][be][much][more][numerical][stable][than] GPK _E_ [ _F, φρ, X, Y_ ].
comments.explanation: Corollary 3.4 introduces GPK'_E without any prior definition. The passage mentions an upper-bound 'GPK'_E' that is supposed to be more numerically stable, but the reader is left guessing how it is constructed (e.g., using SN to rescale). The text before the quote (the corollary statement) is also heavily garbled, so the precise definition is missing. This underspecification makes the numerical stability claim untestable and the corollary incomplete.

comments.title: Contradictory claims about bound tightness for ρ=1
comments.quote: Theoretical study shows that for _ρ_ = 1 case, power-MMD outperforms linear time MMD proposed in Gretton et al. (2012)
comments.explanation: The summary claims theoretical superiority for ρ=1 based on a tighter convergence bound. However, Remark 3.2 explicitly states that the deviation bound for ρ=1 is the same as the existing bound from Gretton et al. (2012). Earlier in Section 5.3.1 the authors assert that they provide a tighter bound, directly contradicting Remark 3.2. The summary does not resolve this inconsistency. A careful reader would be confused about whether the bound is actually tighter or identical, which undermines the key argument for improved performance.

comments.title: Unsupported claim of outperformance
comments.quote: Theoretical study shows that for _ρ_ = 1 case, power-MMD outperforms linear time MMD proposed in Gretton et al. (2012)
comments.explanation: Even if a tighter deviation bound were provided (contradicted by Remark 3.2), the paper does not analyze test power, Type II error, or compare the tests on any data. A tighter bound on the estimator’s deviation does not automatically guarantee better performance in a two‑sample test; the threshold derived from the bound might change the test’s operating characteristics. Without any power analysis or empirical validation, the claim of 'outperforms' is an overstatement of what the theoretical study actually shows.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a novel Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) framework for discrete distributions, aiming to bridge the gap between discrete distribution property estimation (e.g., plugin estimators) and kernel-based hypothesis testing like MMD. While the conceptual motivation is sound and the connection to the power-MMD statistic is intriguing, the current draft suffers from significant readability issues that obscure the technical contribution. The manuscript is plagued by extensive formatting errors and poor typographical presentation—most notably within Definition 2, where mathematical expressions are aggressively indented and merged, rendering the definition nearly illegible. Additionally, the Notation section includes irregular subscripts and spacing ($\_ \

comments.title: Illegible formula in Definition 2
comments.explanation: The formula defining the Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) is severely corrupted by OCR artifacts (represented as `[]`, `_[p][i]`, etc.). While the surrounding text and later definitions suggest it should represent a summation like $\sum_{i,j} \phi(p,q)_i K_{ij} \phi(q,p)_j$ (which corresponds to $\phi(p,q)^T K \phi(q,p)$), the illegibility prevents a rigorous verification of the correctness of indices, transposes, or potential missing terms.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim regarding Probability Product Kernel in Definition 5
comments.quote: The probability product kernel as in (Jebara et al., 2004) is a special case of product GPK where K is a identity matrix.
comments.explanation: Based on the provided text, the 'product GPK' is defined by the mapping function $\phi(\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{p}^l$. When $K = I$, the kernel value becomes $(\mathbf{p}^l)^T (\mathbf{q}^l) = \sum_i p_i^l q_i^l$. However, the Probability Product Kernel (PPK) introduced in (Jebara et al., 2004) is defined as $\int \int \sqrt{P(x)Q(y)P(y)Q(x)} dx dy$ (and its discrete approximation), which involves cross-terms $p_i q_j$ and is not equivalent to the dot product $\sum p_i q_i$ when $K=I$. The claim that the PPK is a special case of their 'product GPK' is therefore incorrect.

comments.title: Theorem 1 Proof: Incorrect Terminology for Matrix Definiteness
comments.quote: K is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, v^T K v \ge 0, where equality holds if and only if v = 0
comments.explanation: The authors refer to 'semipositive definite' (PSD) but then invoke the property of 'positive definite' (PD) matrices—the condition 'equality holds iff v = 0'—to justify the discrepancy property. For a PSD matrix, v can be non-zero while still yielding v^T K v = 0 (e.g., v in the nullspace of K). The Gram matrix K derived from a continuous RKHS over a set of points {v_i} is typically Positive Definite (PD) (strictly positive diagonal), not just Semipositive Definite. This terminology error weakens the rigor of the proof.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Definition 5 regarding the second argument of φ
comments.quote: Definition 5 (product GPK)... the corresponding mapping function is: φ(p, q) = p^l
comments.explanation: The generalized probability kernel (Definition 2) is defined as GPK = (φ(p,q))^T K (φ(q,p)). Definition 5 explicitly defines φ(p,q) as p^l but fails to define φ(q,p). For the claims to hold (e.g., that the probability product kernel is a special case with K=I), the reader must assume an implicit definition, likely φ(q,p) = q^l. While this is a natural assumption for the authors, it is technically an underspecified definition.

comments.title: Undefined notation in proof derivation
comments.quote: obviously, no matter how this element change, it always lies in the finite set of support {v_1, v_2, ..., v_k}... we assume x_s changes its value from v_i to v_{ii}... corresponding count of occurrence N_i changes to N_i - 1 and N_{ii} changes to N_{ii} + 1
comments.explanation: In the proof of Theorem 5 within the Appendix, the authors attempt to illustrate how changing a single sample $x_s$ affects the frequency counts. They introduce a subscript double-$ii$ (e.g., $v_{ii}$ and $N_{ii}$) which is not defined anywhere in the Notation section (which uses $N_i^{(n_1)}$ or $S_i$) or the main text. Based on context, this appears to be a typo for a generic index $j$ (representing the new value the sample moves to). Furthermore, stating the change as "$N_i$ changes to $N_i - 1$" is a confusing algebraic phrasing compared to standard mathematical writing. This notation error obscures the logic for the Lipschitz constant derivation, making the proof step difficult to follow for the reader.

comments.title: Spelling and Grammar Error in Summary
comments.quote: However, bais reduction techniqes proposed in (Yi et al., 2018) and (Yi & Alon, 2020) could be used...
comments.explanation: In the Summary section, there is a clear spelling error: 'bais' is likely intended to be 'bias', and 'techniqes' should be 'techniques'. While these are minor grammatical issues, they slightly reduce the professionalism of the concluding remarks.
